intro: Citrus is grafted, and the rootstock is chosen for your soil and disease pressure, not for which citrus you are growing. Lemons, oranges, mandarins, limes and grapefruit all sit on the same family of citrus rootstocks: trifoliata and its hybrids (the citranges and Swingle citrumelo) for Phytophthora root rot and heavier or replant ground, rough lemon for deep sandy soils, Cleopatra mandarin for shallow alkaline or salty soils, and the contorted dwarf Flying Dragon for a pot. The one trap to know is that some of the best rootstocks, trifoliata, the citranges and Swingle, are incompatible with Eureka lemon, so a Eureka has to go on something else.
sources_note: Rootstock guidance on this page draws on the NSW Agriculture production manual Growing Lemons in Australia and the Citrus Australia rootstock fact sheets, sourced from Auscitrus and NSW Department of Primary Industries research.

sources.name: NSW Agriculture: Growing Lemons in Australia production manual, rootstock chapter (via Citrus Australia)
Citrus Australia: Poncirus trifoliata rootstock fact sheet
Citrus Australia: Flying Dragon rootstock fact sheet
sources.short: Citrus Australia Flying Dragon fact sheet

sources.name: Citrus Australia: Troyer and Carrizo citrange rootstock fact sheet
Citrus Australia: Cleopatra mandarin rootstock fact sheet

rootstocks.name: Poncirus trifoliata
rootstocks.vigour: Small to medium tree, high density
rootstocks.soils: Lime-free fertile clays to loams; cold and wet tolerant, but poor drought tolerance
rootstocks.notes: The disease-resistance benchmark: highly resistant to Phytophthora root rot and citrus nematode, and immune to tristeza virus, with excellent fruit quality. Good for oranges, most mandarins and lemons, but incompatible with Eureka lemon, intolerant of highly acid, alkaline or saline soils, and the slowest of all citrus rootstocks to establish.

rootstocks.name: Flying Dragon
rootstocks.vigour: Very dwarfing, for pots and high density
rootstocks.soils: Cold and wet tolerant, well suited to permanent cultivation in a pot; intolerant of saline, highly acid or alkaline soils
rootstocks.notes: A naturally contorted dwarfing form of trifoliata, the citrus rootstock for a potted or courtyard tree. Assumed to share trifoliata's Phytophthora and tristeza tolerance, with high internal fruit quality, but reduced fruit size and low yield per tree. Good with oranges and grapefruit, less assured with mandarins and lemons, and likely incompatible with Eureka lemon.

rootstocks.name: Troyer / Carrizo citrange
rootstocks.type: Sweet orange x Poncirus trifoliata hybrid
rootstocks.soils: Wide range of well-drained soils, but not heavy clay or high-lime calcareous soils; moderately cold tolerant
rootstocks.notes: The general-purpose citrus rootstock for most varieties: tolerant of Phytophthora, citrus nematode and tristeza (though less than trifoliata), with excellent early fruit quality. Incompatible with Eureka lemon (a yellow ring at the bud union), very prone to micronutrient deficiency on calcareous soils, and prone to sudden death where drainage is poor.

rootstocks.name: Cleopatra mandarin
rootstocks.vigour: Large tree, slow to come into bearing
rootstocks.soils: Shallow alkaline soils and loams; salinity tolerant, but sensitive to waterlogging so needs good drainage
rootstocks.notes: The pick for shallow, alkaline or salty ground, thanks to a shallow root system and its ability to exclude chloride. Good fruit quality but small fruit size, and slow into bearing. Highly sensitive to Phytophthora, so best on virgin (non-replant) soil. Compatible with mandarins, including Imperial, without overgrowth at the bud union.

rootstocks.name: Rough lemon
rootstocks.soils: Best on deep virgin sandy soils; highly drought tolerant, but not for poorly drained or replant soils
rootstocks.notes: The traditional rootstock for deep sandy soils (the relevant one for much of Perth): it makes big, vigorous, high-yielding trees quickly and promotes early maturity, but fruit is coarser, thicker-skinned and lower in juice, and it is susceptible to Phytophthora, nematodes and waterlogging. Best on new ground, not where citrus grew before.

rootstocks.name: Swingle citrumelo
rootstocks.soils: Many soil types, but not clay or highly calcareous ground; good waterlogging and salt tolerance
rootstocks.notes: A trifoliata hybrid that copes with wet feet and salt better than the citranges: Phytophthora, drought and nematode resistant, tristeza tolerant, and good fruit quality. Excellent under grapefruit and good under oranges, but incompatible with Eureka lemon and inclined to overgrow some orange and mandarin varieties.

grow_your_own.intro: Citrus is the one group where most home growers just buy the grafted tree, because the best rootstocks are slow and fiddly to raise. It can still be done: rough lemon and trifoliata are grown from seed, and because much citrus seed is nucellar (the seedlings are clones of the mother plant), a batch comes up uniform and true to the rootstock rather than variable like a peach stone.

sections.heading: Why citrus rootstock comes uniform from seed
sections.body: Many citrus rootstocks are highly nucellar, which means the seed grows into a genetic copy of the parent rather than a cross. That is why nurseries raise Troyer and Carrizo citrange from seed and get uniform, vigorous, upright stocks to bud onto, and it is why a home grafter can raise a usable rootstock from citrus seed and expect it to run true. The catch is that trifoliata is the slowest of all the commercial rootstocks in the nursery, taking six to twelve months longer than the citranges.

sections.heading: Keep the budwood clean
sections.body: Trifoliata and its hybrids are sensitive to viroids such as citrus exocortis, which cause dwarfing and decline, and trifoliata is also sensitive to citrus tatter leaf virus. Budwood for grafting onto these stocks should come from a clean-stock scheme (in Australia, Auscitrus) so the tree is not stunted by disease carried in the scion rather than chosen in the rootstock.

faqs.q: Which citrus rootstock suits sandy Perth soil?
faqs.a: Rough lemon is the traditional choice for deep sandy soils, because it is very drought tolerant and makes a big, fast, high-yielding tree. The trade-offs are coarser, lower-juice fruit and susceptibility to Phytophthora root rot, nematodes and waterlogging, so keep it on well-drained new ground. On heavier soil, or ground where citrus grew before, a Phytophthora-resistant trifoliata or a Troyer or Carrizo citrange is the safer rootstock, and Flying Dragon is the one for a pot.

faqs.q: Why will a Eureka lemon not grow on trifoliata rootstock?
faqs.a: Eureka lemon is incompatible with trifoliata, with the Troyer and Carrizo citranges and with Swingle citrumelo: the bud union fails, often showing as a yellow ring. Eureka has to go on a compatible rootstock instead. Rough lemon suits lemons but wants deep sandy soil, and Benton citrange was bred as the one citrange that works under Eureka without severe incompatibility. Always check rootstock compatibility for the exact citrus variety before grafting.
